name: Pomegranate

book_claims.claim: Can potentially lower inflammation, blood pressure and artery plaque build-up. Small trials suggest may inhibit prostate tumour growth.
book_claims.mechanism: Very rich in polyphenols, especially punicalagin (responsible for ~50% of juice's antioxidant activity). May lower PSA levels.
book_claims.study_ref: Refs 210, 211, 212, 213, 214 in book. Not confirmed in larger trials (ref 215).

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis (Sahebkar et al., 2017) of 8 RCTs found pomegranate juice significantly reduced systolic and diastolic blood pressure, with effects consistent regardless of dose or duration.

Aviram et al. (2004, updated review 2017) found that pomegranate juice consumption for 1-3 years reduced carotid artery intima-media thickness (a measure of atherosclerosis) by up to 30% and reduced oxidized LDL by 90%.

Urolithin A, a gut microbial metabolite of pomegranate ellagitannins, induced mitophagy and improved mitochondrial function in aged mice and in a Phase 1 human trial (Ryu et al., 2016; Andreux et al., 2019).

culinary_pairings.source: Fesenjan (Persian walnut-pomegranate stew); complementary urolithin production
Classic garnish; probiotics may enhance urolithin conversion
Pomegranate molasses with roasted meats; traditional pairing
Fresh herb brightness with pomegranate in salads and drinks
Pomegranate arils in salads; vitamin C aids iron absorption from greens
Both rich in polyphenols; complementary antioxidant activity

seasonality: Northern Hemisphere harvest September-February. Southern Hemisphere March-May. Peak flavour October-January. Arils freeze well. Juice and molasses available year-round.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Responsible for ~50% of juice antioxidant activity. Hydrolysed in gut to ellagic acid, then converted by gut bacteria to urolithins.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Produced by gut bacteria from punicalagins
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Not present in the fruit itself. Requires specific gut bacteria for conversion. Induces mitophagy and improves mitochondrial function. Individual variation in production capacity.
Contribute to red colour and antioxidant activity. Lower than berries per 100g but complementary to punicalagins.
Moderate amount; contributes to total antioxidant capacity alongside polyphenols.
Meaningful contribution; supports blood pressure benefits seen in clinical trials.

synergies.mechanism: Both contain ellagitannins converted to urolithins by gut bacteria; combined intake may enhance urolithin production
Urolithin production depends on gut microbiome composition; probiotics may support the conversion pathway
